actions: Seek rapid diagnostic testing or microscopy before antimalarial treatment when possible.
Urgent medical care is needed for confusion, repeated vomiting, pregnancy, severe weakness, or signs of severe malaria.
Drink fluids and monitor fever while waiting for clinical evaluation.

medications.name: Artemether-lumefantrine
medications.standard_dosage: Common adult regimen: 6-dose course over 3 days; exact dose depends on age and body weight.
medications.administration: Usually taken with food or milk to improve absorption. Must be prescribed or confirmed by a clinician.
warnings: Do not self-medicate without malaria confirmation where testing is available.
Dose varies by weight, pregnancy status, age, and local treatment guidelines.
Severe malaria requires emergency treatment and may need injectable therapy.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for fever or pain relief, not as malaria treatment.
warnings: Avoid overdose and avoid combining multiple products containing paracetamol.
Use caution with liver disease or heavy alcohol use.

actions: Consult a healthcare professional for blood culture or appropriate diagnostic testing.
Maintain hydration and seek urgent care for persistent high fever, confusion, bleeding, or severe abdominal pain.
Antibiotic choice should follow local resistance patterns.

medications.name: Ceftriaxone or azithromycin
medications.standard_dosage: Dose and duration vary by severity, age, pregnancy status, and local antimicrobial resistance.
medications.administration: Antibiotics must be selected and prescribed by a clinician after assessment.
warnings: Do not start antibiotics without medical advice because resistance is common.
Report allergy history before taking any antibiotic.
Severe disease may require hospitalization.

medications.name: Oral rehydration salts
medications.standard_dosage: Use according to sachet instructions after each loose stool or as advised.
medications.administration: Dissolve the full sachet in the correct volume of clean water.
warnings: Incorrect dilution can be harmful.
Seek care if unable to drink, very drowsy, or showing dehydration signs.

actions: Seek clinical evaluation, sputum testing, chest imaging, and public health follow-up.
Wear a mask and reduce close contact if infectious tuberculosis is suspected.
Treatment must be supervised because incomplete therapy causes resistance.

medications.name: Standard TB combination therapy
medications.standard_dosage: Common first-line treatment uses multiple drugs such as isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol; dosing is weight-based.
medications.administration: Must be prescribed, monitored, and completed under a tuberculosis program or qualified clinician.
warnings: Never take single-drug TB treatment or stop early.
Monitoring is needed for liver toxicity, vision effects, interactions, pregnancy, and HIV co-infection.
Possible TB symptoms require medical testing, not symptom-only treatment.

actions: Get confidential HIV testing and counseling if exposure or compatible symptoms are present.
Seek urgent care for suspected recent exposure because post-exposure prophylaxis is time-sensitive.
If positive, treatment and monitoring should start through a qualified HIV care service.

medications.name: Antiretroviral therapy
medications.standard_dosage: Regimen is individualized; common first-line combinations may include tenofovir, lamivudine, and dolutegravir depending on guidelines.
medications.administration: Taken daily only after confirmed diagnosis and clinician selection of an appropriate regimen.
warnings: HIV cannot be diagnosed from symptoms alone.
Drug interactions, kidney function, pregnancy, resistance, and co-infections must be assessed.
Do not share or start antiretroviral medicines without medical supervision.

actions: Rest, drink fluids, and monitor symptoms.
Seek care for breathing difficulty, chest pain, dehydration, pregnancy, very young/older age, or chronic illness.
A clinician may consider antiviral treatment early for high-risk patients.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for fever and body pain relief.
warnings: Avoid overdose and duplicate paracetamol-containing products.
Children, pregnancy, liver disease, and chronic illness require professional advice.

actions: Rest, drink fluids, and consider saline nasal rinses or steam for congestion.
Seek care if symptoms worsen, fever is high, breathing is difficult, or symptoms last more than 10 days.
Antibiotics are not useful for uncomplicated viral colds.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for pain or fever if present.
warnings: Avoid overdose and check other cold medicines for paracetamol.
Children need age- and weight-appropriate dosing from a professional source.

actions: Prioritize oral rehydration and monitor for dehydration.
Seek urgent care for blood in stool, severe dehydration, persistent vomiting, high fever, infants, pregnancy, or elderly patients.
Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless a clinician identifies a bacterial cause.

medications.name: Oral rehydration salts
medications.standard_dosage: Use according to sachet instructions after each loose stool or as advised by a clinician.
medications.administration: Mix the full sachet with the exact amount of clean water printed on the packet.
warnings: Do not dilute incorrectly.
Seek care if unable to keep fluids down or if signs of dehydration appear.

actions: Consider COVID testing according to local availability and guidance.
Isolate or wear a mask while symptomatic to reduce transmission.
Seek urgent care for shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, pregnancy, or high-risk conditions.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for fever, headache, or body pain relief.
warnings: COVID-like symptoms require testing and risk assessment.
Avoid overdose and duplicate paracetamol-containing products.

actions: Seek medical assessment and diagnostic testing if dengue is possible, especially in areas where dengue circulates.
Drink fluids and monitor for warning signs such as severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, rapid breathing, restlessness, or extreme weakness.
Urgent care is needed for bleeding, shock signs, severe dehydration, pregnancy, infants, elderly patients, or worsening symptoms after fever improves.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for fever or pain relief while awaiting clinical evaluation.
warnings: Avoid aspirin, ibuprofen, and other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs unless a clinician says otherwise because dengue can increase bleeding risk.
Do not exceed the recommended paracetamol dose, especially with liver disease or alcohol use.
Dengue has no specific antiviral treatment; severe dengue requires hospital care.

medications.name: Oral rehydration salts
medications.standard_dosage: Use according to sachet instructions or clinician guidance if oral hydration is possible.
medications.administration: Mix the full sachet with the exact amount of clean water printed on the packet.
warnings: Incorrect dilution can be harmful.
Persistent vomiting, dizziness, bleeding, or severe weakness needs urgent medical care.

actions: Treat suspected cholera as urgent because severe dehydration can become life-threatening quickly.
Start oral rehydration while seeking medical care if the patient can drink safely.
Report possible cholera according to local public health guidance and use safe water, sanitation, and hand hygiene measures.

medications.name: Oral rehydration salts
medications.standard_dosage: Use according to sachet instructions; frequent replacement of fluid losses is critical.
medications.administration: Mix the full sachet with the exact amount of clean water printed on the packet.
warnings: Incorrect dilution can be harmful.
Severe dehydration, lethargy, inability to drink, or shock signs require emergency IV fluids.

medications.name: Antibiotics for severe cholera
medications.standard_dosage: Antibiotic choice and dose depend on age, pregnancy status, severity, and local resistance patterns.
medications.administration: Only a clinician or cholera treatment center should decide whether antibiotics are needed.
warnings: Do not use antibiotics for prevention or without clinical guidance.
Rehydration is the most important treatment and should not be delayed.

actions: Seek clinical evaluation for cough with fever, chest pain, confusion, or shortness of breath.
Urgent care is needed for breathing difficulty, blue lips, severe chest pain, confusion, dehydration, infants, elderly patients, pregnancy, or chronic illness.
A clinician may need oxygen assessment, chest examination, imaging, and tests to decide whether antibiotics or hospital care are required.

medications.name: Clinician-selected antibiotic if bacterial pneumonia is suspected
medications.standard_dosage: Antibiotic selection, dose, and duration depend on age, severity, comorbidities, allergies, and local resistance patterns.
medications.administration: Must be prescribed after clinical assessment; viral pneumonia may not need antibiotics.
warnings: Do not self-start leftover antibiotics.
Emergency care is needed for shortness of breath, confusion, chest pain, or oxygen problems.
Children and older adults can worsen quickly.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.standard_dosage: Adults often use 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed; maximum daily dose depends on local guidance and liver risk.
medications.administration: Used for fever or pain relief, not as pneumonia treatment.
warnings: Avoid overdose and duplicate paracetamol-containing products.
Fever relief does not replace evaluation of breathing symptoms.

actions: Possible meningitis is a medical emergency; seek urgent care immediately.
Do not wait at home if fever, severe headache, stiff neck, confusion, photophobia, repeated vomiting, seizure, or infant warning signs are present.
Diagnosis and treatment depend on the cause and may require blood tests, cerebrospinal fluid testing, IV medicines, and hospital monitoring.

medications.name: Emergency hospital treatment
medications.standard_dosage: Treatment depends on the cause; bacterial meningitis often requires urgent IV antibiotics selected by clinicians.
medications.administration: Must be given in a medical facility after urgent assessment.
warnings: Do not try to treat suspected meningitis at home.
Delays can cause death or permanent neurologic injury.
Treatment differs for bacterial, viral, fungal, and other causes.

actions: Seek blood testing for hepatitis B if jaundice, dark urine, abdominal pain, or exposure risk is present.
Avoid alcohol and unnecessary liver-stressing medicines until evaluated by a clinician.
Urgent care is needed for severe abdominal pain, confusion, bleeding, severe vomiting, dehydration, or worsening jaundice.

medications.name: Supportive care for acute hepatitis B
medications.standard_dosage: No specific medicine is usually used for uncomplicated acute hepatitis B; care focuses on hydration, nutrition, and monitoring.
medications.administration: A clinician should confirm diagnosis and monitor liver function.
warnings: Do not self-medicate with herbal or over-the-counter products that may affect the liver.
Paracetamol and other liver-metabolized medicines require clinician advice when hepatitis is suspected.
Vaccination and testing of contacts may be needed.

medications.name: Antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis B
medications.standard_dosage: Medicines such as tenofovir or entecavir may be used for chronic hepatitis B when eligibility criteria are met.
medications.administration: Requires confirmed diagnosis, liver assessment, and long-term clinician monitoring.
warnings: Do not start or stop antiviral treatment without specialist guidance.
Treatment decisions depend on viral load, liver tests, pregnancy status, kidney function, and co-infections.

actions: Call a healthcare provider before going to a clinic if measles is possible because it is highly contagious.
Isolate from others and avoid exposing infants, pregnant people, and immunocompromised people.
Urgent care is needed for breathing difficulty, dehydration, confusion, severe weakness, pneumonia signs, seizures, pregnancy, or very young children.

medications.name: Supportive care
medications.standard_dosage: No specific antiviral treatment is used for routine measles; care focuses on fluids, fever control, and monitoring complications.
medications.administration: Clinical evaluation is needed to confirm measles and manage complications.
warnings: Measles is reportable in many settings; follow local public health instructions.
Do not give aspirin to children or teenagers with viral illnesses.
Complications such as pneumonia, ear infection, diarrhea, and encephalitis require medical care.

medications.name: Vitamin A when clinically indicated
medications.standard_dosage: Vitamin A may be recommended for children with measles in some guidelines; dose depends on age and clinical context.
medications.administration: Use only according to clinician or public health guidance.
warnings: Excess vitamin A can be toxic.
Vaccination status and exposure management should be reviewed by a healthcare professional.

actions: The symptoms are not specific enough for a reliable educational suggestion.
Consult a qualified healthcare professional for examination and diagnostic testing.
Seek urgent care for severe pain, breathing difficulty, confusion, fainting, pregnancy complications, dehydration, or symptoms in young children.
